coredata.dc:title: Facile green synthesis of photoresponsive ZnO/CDs with Lithocarpus pachylepis extract and their antibacterial activity

Ding, Rui
Cai, Shenghao
Hu, Jian
Deng, Jianjian
Miao, Xinxin
Yu, Fen
coredata.dc:description: In this study, ZnO/carbon quantum dots (ZnO/CDs) with enhanced visible light response and photothermal properties were prepared by green synthesis using Lithocarpus pachylepis extract as biological reductants and stabilizers. Compared with pure ZnO, the optimized composite had better solar energy harvesting capacity, which made it could degrade 98% methylene blue within 40 min. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic stability of the composites was validated by cycling tests. More importantly, ZnO/CDs composites can effectively kill bacteria under the synergistic action of photothermy and photocatalysis. This green synthesized photoresponsive ZnO/CDs composites have broad application prospects in the photocatalytic and antibacterial fields, which provide new ideas and strategies to solve the problems of environmental pollution and public health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
Carbon quantum dots
Visible light Photoresponse
Photothermy
Antibacterial property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green synthesis of ZnO and ZnO/CDs
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) production of ZnO/CDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, the inset shows photographs of prepared CDs solutions before and after UV irradiation (Scale bar = 1 cm); (b) The excited wavelength dependent emission spectra of CDs.
(a) XRD spectra and (b) FTIR spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites.
SEM images of ZnO (a) and ZnO/CDs (b) composites.
(a) TEM image of ZnO/CDs; (b) HRTEM image of ZnO/CDs. Insets were HRTEM images with the lattice space of CDs and ZnO; (c) SAED pattern of ZnO/CDs composites.
XPS spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CDs. (a) Survey spectrum; (b) Zn 2p spectrum; (c) O 1 s spectrum; (d) C 1 s spectrum.
PL spectra of synthesized ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites.

(a) UV–vis spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites; (b) (Ahv)
2
vs photon energy plot of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites.

(a-b) UV–vis absorption spectra of MB with ZnO (a) and ZnO/CDs (b) treatment under visible light condition; (c) Degradation curves of MB after treatment with ZnO and ZnO/CDs under visible light irradiation; (d-e) Corresponding optical photos of MB with ZnO (d) and ZnO/CDs (e) treatment in different time periods. (Scale bar = 1 cm); (f) Reaction rate kinetics of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites on MB. Data are means ± standard deviation (SD) (n = 3).
Photocatalytic stability testing of ZnO/CDs composites in four reaction cycles.

Photothermal conversion performance of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites under NIR irradiation. (a) Heating curves of ZnO and ZnO/CDs under NIR (808 nm, 0.5 W/cm2, 25 min) irradiation. The control group was PBS; (b) Temperature changes of ZnO/CDs for four NIR on/off cycles; (c) Thermal images of PBS, ZnO, and ZnO/CDs composites under NIR irradiation (Scale bar = 1 cm). Data are means ± SD (n = 3).
Fluorescence intensity of DCF in PBS (Control), ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites under visible light irradiation.

(a-b) Photographs of inoculated bacterial colonies after different treatments of S. aureus (a) and E. coli (b) (Scale bar = 2 cm); (c-d) Antibacterial rates of ZnO and ZnO/CDs on S. aureus (c) and E. coli (d) under 808 nm NIR and visible light irradiation. Data are means ± SD (n = 3). * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001.
The schematic mechanism for improved photodegradation and antibacterial efficacy of ZnO/CDs composites.
Kinetic equation and parameters for the degradation of MB solution with different photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Facile green synthesis of photoresponsive ZnO/CDs with Lithocarpus pachylepis extract and their antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China Jiaotong University, Nanchang, Jiangxi, 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China Jiaotong University, Nanchang, Jiangxi, 330013, P.R.China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi, 330006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi, 330006, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, ZnO/carbon quantum dots (ZnO/CDs) with enhanced visible light response and photothermal properties were prepared by green synthesis using Lithocarpus pachylepis extract as biological reductants and stabilizers. Compared with pure ZnO, the optimized composite had better solar energy harvesting capacity, which made it could degrade 98% methylene blue within 40 min. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic stability of the composites was validated by cycling tests. More importantly, ZnO/CDs composites can effectively kill bacteria under the synergistic action of photothermy and photocatalysis. This green synthesized photoresponsive ZnO/CDs composites have broad application prospects in the photocatalytic and antibacterial fields, which provide new ideas and strategies to solve the problems of environmental pollution and public health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the present era of rapid technological advancement, issues such as environmental pollution [1] and bacterial infections [2] are becoming increasingly severe. Hence, there is a growing urgency for efficient and sustainable solutions. Research focused on light-responsive materials has garnered significant attention due to their potential in addressing these problems [3]. It is worth noting that metal oxides have been widely applied in photocatalytic field due to their excellent ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) light absorption property [4], biosecurity [5], chemical stability [6] and affordability [7]. Among them, as a typical photoresponsive material, ZnO has become a research hot because of its favorable electrical and optical properties, high electron mobility, natural abundance, flexibility in synthesis [8]. What's more, ZnO has been approval as an inorganic antibacterial agent by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [9]. However, there are still certain limitations of single ZnO in practical applications, such as the narrow photoresponsive range and the tendency for complexation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which restrict its practical applications [10]. Previous studies have found that the visible light responsive properties of ZnO can be enhanced by reducing charge carrier complexation through doping metal/non-metal atoms [11], depositing noble metals [12], constructing heterojunctions [13], coupling carbon materials [14] and surface photosensitization [15].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have shown excellent photoluminescence properties, high fluorescence activity, chemical inertness and stability due to optical stability and quantum confinement effect, and have gained wide attention in various applications [16–19]. CDs possess both up-conversion luminescence and down-conversion luminescence properties, as well as photo-induced transport properties and long-wavelength light trapping ability, which make them a potential high-performance photocatalytic material [16,20]. Photo-induced CDs are excellent electron donors and acceptors, and this ability is the main factor for improving the photocatalytic performance. The response ability of CDs to the solar spectrum, enhance the adsorption capacity of the composite system, and resist photogenerated electron-hole pairs are emerging as an important player in photocatalytic applications [21]. The combination of CDs with different semiconductor materials such as ZnS [22], TiO2 [23,24], Fe3O4 [25,26], Au NPs [27] has been widely studied. It has been shown that the modification of semiconductor materials with CDs can be improved visible light photocatalysis, and the combination of CDs and ZnO is expected to construct photocatalytic composites with superior properties. However, the current conventional synthesis synthesis of ZnO, CDs and ZnO/CDs composites is mainly chemical synthesis, which has some disadvantages such as high cost, high impurity content, instability, limited reproducibility and reliability. In addition, chemical synthesis often requires the use of toxic chemicals that are harmful to the environment and medical applications, while inevitably producing some toxic by-products.

In the last decades, research on green synthesis processes has been developed extensively due to industrial safety, environmental concerns, and sustainability considerations, making it an attractive alternative to traditional synthetic processes [28]. Compared with traditional synthesis methods, the technology of synthesizing nanomaterials using plant extracts is more environmentally friendly and sustainable [29]. Green synthesis of ZnO nanoparticles using plant extracts as reducing and capping agents has gained attention in the recent years [30]. Some components of plant extracts also act as stabilizers, chelators and capping agents, which help in the synthesis and stabilisation of nanoparticles. Moreover, some bioactive components in plant extracts are attached to the surface of ZnO, giving them special properties, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and antibacterial properties [31,32]. Lithocarpus pachylepis is rich in protein, vitamin A and other key nutrients. It contains active ingredients that can effectively antibacteria and inhibit inflammation. More importantly, Lithocarpus pachylepis contains mainly flavonoids, polyphenols and terpenoids that control the nucleation and agglomeration of nanomaterials [33]. Therefore, theoretically, it can be used as a reducing agent for green synthesis and improve the biological properties of the prepared ZnO.

In view of the advantages of nanocomposites and sustainable chemical properties, ZnO/CDs composites were prepared by green synthesis method using water extract of Lithocarpus pachylepis (Scheme 1 ). This process was carried out using aqueous extract of Lithocarpus pachylepis as a reducing and capping agent, which reduced the production of by-products. The ZnO/CDs showed excellent performance in terms of both visible light responsive and photothermal responsive properties. This not only improved the utilization efficiency of solar energy, but also effectively degraded organic pollutants such as methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. It is of great significance for environmental protection and water purification, and also expands its application potential in many fields. In addition, the doping of CDs greatly improved its photothermal conversion performance, and under near-infrared (NIR) irradiation, the temperature can be raised to 55°C in 10 min. For antibacterial applications, the material generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) through light response and combines with photothermal effect to form a unique antibacterial mechanism. Under the synergistic effect of photothermal and photocatalytic therapy, bacteria can be effectively killed by ZnO/CDs. Therefore, the research of photoresponsive ZnO/CDs composites provides a new idea and method for solving the problems of environmental pollution and bacterial infection.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), aqueous ammonia (NH3·H2O) were purchased from Xilong Scientific (Guangdong, China). Methylene blue (MB), l-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Lithocarpus pachylepis was purchased from Taobao. 2′, 7′ -dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). The procured chemicals were processed without further modifications.

A facile one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CDs [34]. Typically, 0.2 g l-ascorbic acid was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water solution to form a homogeneous l-ascorbic acid solution, which was then heated in a hair dryer at 150°C for 5 h. Subsequently, the solution cooled to room temperature was placed in a dialysis bag for 3 days. Finally, the dialyzed solution was centrifuged at 7500 r/min for 5 min, and the supernatant was the CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of ZnO and ZnO/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 8 g of Lithocarpus pachylepis was taken in 200 mL of deionized water and stirred in a water bath at 65°C for 1 h. The supernatant was centrifuged from the above solution to obtain the aqueous extract of Lithocarpus pachylepis. Then, 8 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 30 mL of CDs solution were sequentially added to the extract. Subsequently, the pH of the mixture was regulated to 10 with ammonia, and the reaction was performed in a magnetic stirrer at a constant temperature of 85°C for 8 h. After that, the precipitation after centrifugation was washed 3 times with deionized water and dried in the oven. Finally, ZnO/CDs composite powder was obtained by calcination at 400°C in a tube furnace for 2 h. Similarly, ZnO was prepared through the above method.

The phase components of ZnO/CDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker GMBH, Germany). The surface morphology of the samples was studied through scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, Germany). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Jem-2100F) was employed to analyze the internal morphology of sample. The surface electronic states of ZnO/CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The light absorption properties of ZnO/CDs were characterized using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimazu, Japan). The photoluminescence spectra of the samples were determined using RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of ZnO/CDs was determined using PerkinElmer infrared spectrometer (Platinum Elmer).

The photocatalytic performance of ZnO/CDs was investigated by photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB). In the photocatalytic process, the photocatalyst (1 g/L) was dispersed in MB solution (5 mg/L), which was ultrasonicated for 5 min and stirred in the dark under constant conditions (250 rpm, 25°C) for 30 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, 300 W) was used to irradiate the solution to induce the photocatalytic reaction. The distance from the lamp to the liquid surface was 15 cm. Subsequently, 3 mL of MB solution was removed from the reactor every 10 min and the supernatant was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. And the absorbance of MB solution at 665 nm was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The rate of photocatalytic degradation was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
Degradation
rate
%
=
A 0
− A
/ A × 100 %
where A
o is the initial concentration of the MB at t
0 , and A is the concentration of the MB at t.

5 mL ZnO/CDs (0.04 g/mL) composites suspension was was irradiated with NIR (0.5 W/cm2, 808 nm), and the temperature of the solution was monitored and recorded by the thermometer every 2 min for 25 min. The temperature of the phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and the ZnO solution was monitored and recorded in the same way. Finally, ZnO/CDs composites (0.04 g/mL) were subjected to a shutdown/cooling cycle with a 0.5 W/cm2 laser for 25 min and repeated 4 times.

The ROS generation from ZnO/CDs was detected by ROS sensitive probe (DCFH-DA) [35]. Briefly, fluorescent dye solutions incorporating ZnO, ZnO/CDs composites, and PBS were subjected to visible light irradiation, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was monitored by emission wavelength of 525 nm and excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of ZnO/CDs in vitro. The bacterial suspension was diluted with PBS to 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, and then the sample (1 mg) was added into 10 mL the diluted bacterial solution and shaken well. The bacterial solution was irradiated by 808 nm NIR for 10 min and xenon lamp for 5 min, and then co-cultivated at 37°C for 4 h. Each experiment was performed 3 times in parallel. The antibacterial rate was calculated by counting bacterial colonies as following Eq. (2).
(2)
Antibacterial
rate
%
=
N 0
− N
/ N × 100 %
where N
0 presents the number of bacterial colony in the control group, and N is the number of bacterial colony in the experimental groups.

Firstly, the optical characteristic of the synthesized CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1a, the absorption band of 220–280 nm was the π-π* electron transition, corresponding to the CC bond. The absorption band at 320–400 nm was the n-π* electron transition, which associated with the CO bond [36]. In addition, under UV irradiation, CDs exhibit blue fluorescence. To study the wavelength dependence of the CDs, the fluorescence spectra were measured at different excitation wavelengths of 320–390 nm (Fig. 1b). As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission intensity and fluorescence intensity were decreased gradually, which was the wavelength dependence of CDs [37]. The above results indicated the synthesized carbon nanoparticles were CDs.

The XRD spectra of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were displayed in Fig. 2a. The characteristic peaks of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites were located at 31.84°, 34.48°, 36.32°, 47.57°, 56.64°, 62.91°, 68°, and 69.2°, which corresponding to the hexagonal wurtzite ZnO (JCPDS 36–1451) [38]. However, perhaps due to the lower content of CDs in ZnO/CDs composites, no obvious characteristic peaks of CDs were detected [39].

The molecular structure and chemical composition of the samples were studied through FTIR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the bands at 1615 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 were associated with asymmetrical and symmetrical vibrations of O–C–O. The strong absorption band at 463 cm−1 was caused by ZnO stretching vibration [40].

According to Fig. 3 , both ZnO and ZnO/CDs were rod-like structures. Unfortunately, due to the low resolution of SEM analysis, CDs could not be obviously observed in the SEM images [38]. Therefore, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with higher resolution was needed for further observation.

As seen from Fig. 4a, the average particle size of ZnO/CDs was about 100 nm, and the nanoparticles had obvious agglomeration. The high-resolution TEM images (HRTEM) of ZnO/CDs were displayed in Fig. 4b. As shown in the insets, the lattice spacing of 0.13 nm was closely matched the (013) spacing of carbon, while the lattice spacing of 0.26 nm was corresponds to the distance between the (002) facets in the ZnO lattice [41]. Fig. 4c shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of ZnO/CDs. The polycrystalline diffraction ring proves the polycrystalline properties of ZnO/CDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that ZnO/CDs were successfully synthesized.

The chemical structures of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were further determined by XPS. Fig. 5a exhibits Zn, C and O elements in ZnO/CDs (Fig. 5 a1), while there are only Zn and O elements in ZnO (Fig. 5 a2). The characteristic peaks at 1044 eV and 1021 eV were related to Zn (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, the reason for the appearance of ZnO bonds at 530.6 eV was the O2− in the ZnO bond of ZnO wurtzite structure. After deconvolution of the spectrum, the OH peak appeared at 531.8 eV, which was related to the absorption of OH groups on the ZnO surface. The CO bond formed by adsorption on the CDs surface was mainly located at 533 eV (Fig. 5 c1). However, since ZnO is not incorporated into CDs, this characteristic peak does not exist (Fig. 5 c2). The C 1 s peak could be deconvoluted into three peaks, and their binding energies were 288.2 eV, 286.5 eV and 284.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 5d). The above results could confirm the successful synthesis of ZnO/CDs [42].

In order to study the transfer, migration, and recombination of photogenerated electron pairs in photocatalysts, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy is usually required [43]. PL analysis results showed (Fig. 6 ) that the photoluminescence intensity of ZnO/CDs composites was significantly decreased compared with ZnO, which proved that the addition of CDs can greatly reduce the photorecombination of electrons and holes. This may be explained by the fact that CDs can be served as electron acceptor, thereby enhancing the transient photoelectron response of ZnO/CDs and reducing the photoluminescence intensity. The good electrical conductivity of CDs can promote the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. With the aid of light, the photogenerated electrons produced by CDs could be transferred to other substances quickly to participate in the redox reaction, while the holes remain on the surface of CDs and continue to participate in the reaction, thus improving the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction [44]. When composited with other semiconductor materials, CDs can be used as an electron transfer medium to accelerate electron transfer and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, which further improves the photocatalytic performance of ZnO/CDs [45]. Therefore, ZnO/CDs had better photocatalytic activity than ZnO theoretically.

The optical properties of ZnO/CDs were evaluated by UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 7a indicated that the absorption range of ZnO/CDs in the visible light region was obviously wider than that of ZnO. Subsequently, the energy band gaps of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were measured by Tauc plot method [46]. As revealed in Fig. 7b, the energy band gaps of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were 3.18 eV and 2.79 eV, respectively. This result indicated that ZnO/CDs energy band gap was effectively reduced by the combination with CDs. The relatively narrow energy band gap of ZnO/CDs would increase the photodegradation efficiency of organic dyes in visible light.

For the purpose of studying the photocatalytic performance of ZnO/CDs, the degradation process of MB was simulated with the aid of visible light. The photocatalytic degradation results indicated that the ZnO/CDs composites could degrade about 95% of MB for 40 min, while ZnO needed 90 min to degrade approximately 78% of MB (Fig. 8a-c). Through Fig. 8d-e, the color changes of MB during degradation were clearly observed. Moreover, at 40 min, the color of MB treated with ZnO/CDs was almost disappeared, indicating that MB was effectively degraded at this time. These results revealed that ZnO/CDs possessed significantly higher photocatalytic activity compared with pure ZnO.

During the photocatalytic degradation process, the ZnO/CDs composites exhibited a better degradation effect on MB, which may be attributed to effectively separated the photogeneratede-h pairs. According to previous reports, due to the electrical interaction between ZnO and CDs, the photogenerated charge separation can be improved, and the transfer of e−/h+ pairs can be enhanced [47]. The enhancement of the interaction between CDs electrons and ZnO conduction band (CB) leads to the upconversion mainly because the optimal value of CDs is lower than the conduction band of ZnO [39]. The dye degradation rate constants (k) of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were determined using the quasi-first order kinetic equation Eq. (3).
(3)
ln
C 0
/ C
= kt

In the MB degradation process, the irradiation time was linearly corresponding to ln(C0/C) (Fig. 8f). The degradation curve obtained by pseudo-primary kinetics was correct. The rate constants were shown in Table 1 , and we can see that the rate constant of ZnO/CDs composites was 0.08411 min−1, R2 was 0.98519, and the rate constant of ZnO was 0.01263 min−1, R2 = 0.97897. In sum, compared with ZnO, the rate constant of ZnO/CDs composites was higher, which was consistent with the photodegradation results.

Photocatalytic stability is an important property of photocatalyst in practical applications. Therefore, a cyclic test of MB degradation was performed using ZnO/CDs composites (Fig. 9 ). The results demonstrated that ZnO/CDs composites exhibited excellent photocatalytic stability, and even after four cycles of use, there was no significant deactivation phenomenon. The reduction in degradation efficiency may be related to the rate of mass loss of the photocatalyst in each cycle.

First, warming effects of PBS, ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites were evaluated under NIR irradiation. After irradiation for 25 min, the solution temperatures of PBS, ZnO and ZnO/CDs were increased to 29°C, 41°C and 65°C (Fig. 10a). This result indicated that ZnO/CDs had good photothermal performance. Moreover, there was no obvious temperature attenuation in the cyclic heating curve, which demonstrated that ZnO/CDs composites possessed high photothermal stability (Fig. 10b). As intuitively seen from the NIR thermographic images (Fig. 10c), the temperature of the ZnO/CDs composites solution gradually increased under NIR irradiation. The above result indicated that the ZnO/CDs composites solution had good photothermal conversion performance and was expected to realize photothermal sterilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) production of ZnO/CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, DCFH-DA were utilized to detect ROS generation from ZnO/CDs. After exposure to visible light, non-fluorescent DCFH can be oxidized to fluorescent DCF by the generated ROS. Therefore, the amount of ROS produced by ZnO/CDs could be reflected through testing the fluorescence intensity of DCF. As displayed in Fig. 11 , compared with ZnO and PBS group, the DCF fluorescence intensity in ZnO/CDs was significantly higher. This result manifested that the composite of CDs promoted the ROS production of ZnO. This may be due to the fact that CDs in nanocomposites act as effective absorbers, inhibiting the electron hole pair complexation caused by light.

On the basis of above results, the antibacterial effect of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites against S. aureus and E. coli in vitro was studied under 808 nm NIR and visible light. Results showed that the sterilization efficiency of ZnO/CDs was not ideal (Fig. 12a-b), which may be caused by its low working concentration (100 μg/mL). Even with the help of photocatalysis or photothermal action, it was still unable to completely kill bacteria. However, under the synergistic effect of photothermal therapy (PTT) and photocatalytic therapy (PCT), all bacteria were killed after being treated with ZnO/CDs. Subsequently, the antibacterial rates were calculated according to the number of bacterial colonies (Fig. 12c-d). Under the action of PTT, the antibacterial rates of ZnO and ZnO/CDs against S. aureus reached about 45% and 75% and against E. coli reached about 45% and 75%, respectively. While under the action of PCT, the inhibition rates against S. aureus were 36% (ZnO) and 75% (ZnO/CDs) respectively, and the inhibition rates against E. coli were 36% (ZnO) and 50% (ZnO/CDs) respectively. Worth mentioning that under the synergistic act in of PTT/PCT, the inhibitory effects of ZnO and ZnO/CDs composites on S. aureus were about 75.6% and 99.6%, respectively, and on E. coli were about 60% and 99.8%. These results manifested that the addition of CDs could not only improve the visible light utilization efficiency of ZnO, but also enhance its photothermal performance, thereby improving its antibacterial performance through the synergistic effect of PTT and PCT.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of ZnO/CDs was shown in Fig. 13 . First, CDs, which act as an electron reservoir, create photoreactive sites by hindering charge recombination, thus helping to separate light-generated electron-hole pairs [48,49]. After providing a band gap energy higher than or equal to the photocatalyst, once the electron-hole pairs are generated, this energy level could promote the transfer of electrons from the CB of ZnO to the charge-transfer state close to the CDs, which effectively separate the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and hinder charge recombination to produce photoreactive sites [48]. On the other hand, the p-electrons of carbon have stronger electronic coupling to the CB of ZnO. This is because the CDs has an optimal value of 4.5 eV, which is just lower than the CB energy of 4.05 eV of ZnO, resulting in an upconversion and extending the absorption capacity to a longer wavelength region [50]. Meanwhile, when the hole stays near the valence band of ZnO, a large number of photoresponsive sites would be created, which contribute to generate energetic radicals. For example, the superoxide radical (∙O2 −) can be generated by the reaction of the electron rich on CDs and ZnO with the adsorbed O molecule. At the same time, when these holes migrate to the surface, they would react with H2O to form hydroxides (∙OH) and with superoxide protonates to form peroxides (∙OOH). Overall, these high-energy free radicals can break up macromolecule into smaller ones. Thus, the superior photocatalytic activity owned by ZnO/CDs is mainly attributed to the joint action of CDs and ZnO [50]. These reactions can be summarized in the following Eq. (4)–(12):
(4)
ZnO / CDs + hv →
e −
CDs
+
h +
ZnO
(5)
e −
+
O 2
→ ⋅
O 2 −
(6)
h +
+
OH −
→ ⋅ OH
(7)
O 2 −
+
H 2
O →
HO 2 −
+
OH −
(8)
HO 2 −
+
H 2
O →
H 2
O 2
+ ⋅ OH
(9)
H 2
O 2
→ 2 ⋅ OH
(10)
h +
+ dye →
CO 2
+
H 2
O
(11)
⋅ OH + dye →
CO 2
+
H 2
O
(12)
⋅
O 2 −
+ dye →
CO 2
+
H 2
O

In summary, ZnO/CDs composites with enhanced visible light response and good photothermal properties were prepared by green synthesis method. A series of analyses showed that the incorporation of CDs could effectively improve the separation efficiency of photocarrier and significantly promote the degradation of organic pollutants. Compared with ZnO, ZnO/CDs displayed better performance in degradation of organic pollutants with the aid of visible light, and has important application value in environmental treatment field. In addition, it also possessed good photothermal properties and photothermal stability. Through the synergistic effect of PTT and PCT, ZnO/CDs exhibited good killing effect on common bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli). To sum up, the photoresponsive ZnO/CDs composites successfully synthesized in this study exhibit good performance in both photocatalysis and antibacterial field, which provide a promising material and technology pathway to solve the environmental pollution and hygiene problems.

Yang Xiao: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rui Ding: Methodology, Investigation. Shenghao Cai: Validation. Jian Hu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Jianjian Deng: Software, Data curation. Xinxin Miao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Fen Yu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Natural Science Youth Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20242BAB20277 to Fen Yu, 20224BAB216032 to Xinxin Miao), Key research and development plan of Jiangxi Province. (20243BBI91009 to Xinxin Miao).
